text:
## Experimental

### Materials synthesis

PSHC was prepared by a two-step synthesis approach, including hydrothermal treatment at a relatively low temperature and carbonization at a set of high temperatures. Figure 1 shows the schematic diagram of the synthesis route. Firstly, pistachio shells waste was collected from pistachio nuts which were purchased in the local market, washed with distilled water and ethanol by ultrasonic cleaning, and dried at 80 °C for 12 h in an electric oven. The first step was to immerse 4 g cleaned pistachio shells waste into 3 M NaOH solutions with a volume of 80 mL in a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. Then the autoclave was sealed and placed in an electric oven at 165 °C for 6 h under the hydrothermal treatment that aimed at removing part of the lignin and hemicellulose of pistachio shells waste. After that, the samples were collected by vacuum filtration and washed by distilled water and ethanol several times until the pH value of the solution reached above 6, and then dried at 80 °C for 12 h. After the drying process, the fluffy and the pale pink precursor was obtained (Figure S1). Finally, the prepared precursor was carbonized at a set of temperatures from 800 to 1100 °C in a tube furnace under Ar flow for 2 h (a heating rate of 10 °C min−1) to obtain the black PSHC-X (X was the pyrolysis temperature) powders (Figure S1). As a comparison, we also prepared the direct pyrolysis carbon without any further treatment at 800–1100 °C under the same pyrolysis time (termed: PSDC-X).

### Materials characterization

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a thermal analysis instrument (Netzsch STA 449 F3) with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 (from room temperature to 1000 °C) in N2 atmosphere. X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker D8) analyses were performed at 40 kV with Cu Kα radiation. Raman spectroscopy (Bruker VERTEX 70) was utilized to analyze the bonding characteristics and the existence of defects of the obtained materials. Nitrogen sorption analysis was carried out with a Brunauer–Emmett–Teller surface area analyzer (BET, BeiShiDe 3H-2000PM) at a liquid nitrogen temperature. Before analysis, all the materials were degassed under vacuum at 200 °C for 6 h. The pore size distribution was derived from desorption branch isotherms and the pore size was calculated based on Horvaih–Kawazoe (H–K) equation. The morphology of the samples were investigated by field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, MIRA3, TESCAN) and high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100F).

### Electrochemical measurements

The electrode slurry was prepared by mixing the as-prepared materials, Super P and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder in a weight ratio of 8:1:1 dissolved in N-methyl-2pyrrolidinine (NMP) solvent, following by stirring for 2 h into a homogeneous condition. The slurry was uniformly pasted onto copper foil, and then dried in a vacuum oven at 120 °C for 12 h to manufacture the working electrode.

Electrochemical tests were performed with CR2025 type coin cell with sodium foil as the counter electrode and a Glass fiber membrane (Whatman) as the separator. Assembling of the coin cells was operated in an argon-filled glove box (Mbraun, Germany). The electrolyte was 1 M NaClO4 dissolved in ethylene carbonate (EC) and propylene carbonate (PC) in a volume ratio of 1:1. The coin cells were galvanostatically charged and discharged in a battery analyzers (LAND, Wuhan, China) over a range of 2.0–0.001 V versus Na/Na+ at different constant current density. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) tests were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, CHI660E) at a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analyses were conducted with an applied initial voltage of 5 mV in the range of 100 kHz–0.01 Hz. All the electrochemical tests were carried out at room temperature.

## Experimental section

### Sample preparation

The as-received apricot shells were firstly washed and then broken into small particles by mechanical milling. The direct pyrolysis process was conducted at various temperatures (900 °C, 1100 °C, 1300 °C and 1500 °C). The obtained hard carbons were named as HAS-900, HAS-1100, HAS-1300 and HAS-1500, respectively. After further broken into smaller particles, the pyrolyzed carbons were reduced under the H2/Ar hybrid atmosphere (7: 93 in volume) at 800 °C for 2 h. The fianal hard carbons were named as HASH-900, HASH-1100, HASH-1300 and HASH-1500, respectively.

### Materials characterization

SEM and TEM images were captured by a Hitachi S-4800 microscope and a JEM-2100F microscope, respectively. FT-IR spectra was recorded by a FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet Magna-IR 560). The microcrystalline structure variation was investigated by XRD (Rigaku D/Max 2500) and Raman spectra (Renishaw MKI-2000). An ASAP 2020 analyzer was used to measure the porosity of the samples by Nitrogen adsorption. Brunauere-Emmette-Teller (BET) theory and density functional theory (DFT) tests were performed to measure the specific surface area and pore size distribution.

### Electrochemical measurements

A mixture composed of active material, carbon black (Super P, IMERYS) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (HSV900, ARKEMA) in N-methypyrrolidine (NMP) (80:10:10 wt %) was coated on Cu foil and dried at 80 °C overnight to obtain the working electrodes with an active mass loading of ∼1.2 mg cm−2. The working electrodes, separator (glass fiber, Grade GF/D, whatman), electrolyte (1 M NaClO4 dissolved in ethylene carbonate (EC) and diethyl carbonate (DEC) (1:1 in volume), NC-009, Fosai)) were assembled under argon atmosphere in a glove box to obtain the 2430-type working coin cells. The amount of electrolyte is ca. 0.20 ml for one coin cell. The electrochemical performance was evaluated on the CHI660E electrochemical measurement system and the LAND CT2001A battery-testing instrument.

## Experimental

### Material synthesis

The olive leaves are directly collected from the Marche region, Italy. The olive leaves were washed with distilled water and dried at 80 °C for 3 days in an oven. The raw material was smashed and immersed in the solution of dilute hydrochloric acid (HCl) for three days to obtain a more porous structure and to remove any residual impurities (leaves and HCl with a weight ratio of 1:3). Afterward, the product was washed using ultrapure water several times until pH = 7 was obtained and then stored at 80 °C overnight. Olive leaves-Derived Hard Carbon, hereafter designated as OLDHC, was prepared by the pyrolysis of the product in a tubular furnace at 970 °C for 6 h under argon atmosphere after a heating ramp of 5 °C/min [] (the pyrolysis yield was ∼ 24%). The mentioned temperature was used because small molecules, such as H2O, CO2, and N2 among others, are released, resulting in high porosity materials, and low particle density for a heat treatment temperature near 1000 °C []. At higher temperatures (>1200 °C) the nature of the pores could be less open, leading to decreased BET (Brunauer, Emmett and Teller) surface areas [] and resulting in lower performance in energy storage devices. Besides, specific surface area and pore-size distribution are the two most essential aspects influencing the performance of carbonaceous materials in EDLCs [], therefore, HCs prepared for this purpose were heated near 1000 °C [,,]. Ultimately, the resultant powder was milled in a Retsch S100 ball mill with a speed of 400 rpm for 4 h (agate jar of 50 mL and agate ball diameter 10 mm, ball-to-powder ratio = 4:1 in weight).

### Material characterization

The structure of the OLDHC powder was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Bragg–Brentano geometry, Cu-Kα, λ = 1.54059 Å) and Raman spectra (Horiba IHR 320, wavelength 532 nm). From XRD, the interlayer spacing (d002), the stacking height (Lc), and the graphene stacking layers number (n) were defined. The d002 was calculated based on Bragg's law: d002=λ/2sin(θ002), with λ = 0.154 nm, while the Lc was obtained from Scherrer's equation: Lc=0.9λ/β002cos(θ002), where β002 is equal to the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 002 peak. n was obtained by dividing Lc by d002 [,]. By Raman spectra, the graphitic domain size (La) was revealed from La=(2.4∗10−10)λ4(ID/IG)−1, with λ = 532 nm [,]. The Fityk software was used to perform background removal and normalization. The deconvolution of the spectra was carried out by using Lorent-zian-Gaussian fitting on Spec5 software. The morphology of the samples and the distribution of elements were explored by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis using a FE-SEM Cambridge Stereoscan 360 electron microscope coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX). The Infra-Red spectra of the pristine olive leaves were carried out using a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum Two FTIR spectrometer within the wave number range of 500–4000 cm−1. To study pore characteristics, nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm was performed at −195.8 °C in a Micrometritics ASAP 2020 analyzer on a sample preliminarily outgassed for 12 h at 200 °C. The specific surface area values were calculated by the linear plot in the relative pressure range.

X-ray Photoemission Spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were done using an in-house XPS apparatus. The powder was shaped into pellets and was introduced through a specially designed load lock chamber into the measurement chamber. For measurement, a focused Al Kα radiation (hν = 1486.6 eV) with the base chamber pressure of 10−9 mbar was utilized. To confirm the homogeneity of the sample as well as radiation degradation prevention, different points on the sample were examined with a short dwell time. Core peaks were evaluated via a non-linear Shirley background.

### Electrode preparation and electrochemical tests for LIBs and NIBs

The working electrode was prepared by mixing the OLDHC active material with conductive carbon SuperC-65 and Na-carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) (Sigma-Aldrich) binder (80:10:10 in weight) in deionized water, then coating the acquired slurry onto Cu foil for LIBs and onto Al foil for NIBs by using a doctor blade. After drying the laminates at room temperature overnight, the anode electrodes were cut and punched and then vacuum dried in BUCHI Glass Oven Drying at 120 °C for 10 h. The main purpose of vacuum-drying is to allow the removal of moisture under vacuum. OLDHC-based electrodes were evaluated in half cells using either metallic Li or Na, correspondingly, both as the counter and reference electrode in T-shape polypropylene Swagelok-type 3 cells. Assembly was performed in an argon-filled glove box (Jacomex GP-campus, oxygen, and moisture content less than 0.8 ppm). 1 M LiPF6 in a mixture of 1:1 (v/v) ethylene carbonate (EC)/dimethyl carbonate (DMC) (preformulated by Solvionic, France) electrolyte was used for LIBs, while 1 M NaClO4 (Sigma-Aldrich) in EC: PC (Sigma-Aldrich) electrolyte was used for NIBs. For both LIBs and NIBs, a glass fiber disk (Whatman GF/A) was utilized as separator. The electrochemical behavior of the electrodes was characterized by a VMP-3 multichannel electrochemical workstation (Bio-Logic, France). All the cells were kept 12 h at open circuit voltage (OCV) before running the measurements. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) was obtained in the potential range within 0.01–3.00 V at 0.05 mV/s scan rate. All potential values are given vs. Li+/Li and Na+/Na redox couples for LIBs and NIBs, respectively. GCPL tests of the electrodes were collected in the same potential range at 1C = 300 mA/g with respect to active material mass. In addition, the C-rate capability of the electrode was tested in the C/10 to 2C range (5 cycles at every rate), and capacity recovery was subsequently evaluated at C/10 for LIBs and NIBs. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were carried out at each 10th cycle at E = 0.5 V bias potential, with an AC amplitude of 10 mV, in the frequency range 10 mHz < f < 100 kHz. The GITT measurements were carried out at C/10 with pulse time τ = 0.5 h and relaxation period of 2 h.

### Electrode preparation and electrochemical test for EDLCs

To make a slurry, the same procedure has been applied for EDLC electrodes, by using Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) binder dissolved in a N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) (both chemicals from Sigma-Aldrich) slurry. The mixed slurry was uniformly coated onto a nickel foam current collector and heated at 70 °C for 2 h and then dried at room temperature. After drying, the electrode was pressed using a rolling press. The electrochemical behavior of the OLDHC electrode was tested via a standard three-electrode system in 6.0 M KOH aqueous solution using a SP-240 electrochemical workstation (Bio-Logic, France). A Pt foil and an Ag/AgCl electrode served as the counter and reference electrodes, respectively. The cyclic voltammetry (CV) was tested between −1.2 V and 0 V vs. SCE at the scan rates of 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100 mV/s. The charge/discharge behavior was evaluated at different current densities between −1.2 V and 0 V vs. SCE, while the cycling behavior of the electrode was estimated at 10 A/g up to more than 20,000 cycles. EIS measurements were carried out in the frequency range from 10 mHz to 100 kHz, with an AC amplitude of 10 mV over OCV.
